data.title: Trump Is Pushing For A Rate Cut: Is Powell Really Too Late?
data.description:
Intro

President Trump has tried in every way to say that he would like a rate cut, but Powell (or more generally the FOMC) still seems reluctant at the moment. Actually, the more time passes, the less willing he seems to agree with Trump's opinion.

A month ago, the probability that the Fed would not cut rates in mid-June was 72.40%, while today it has risen to 95.60%. Yet unemployment is low, inflation is under control, and major US companies are posting record profits. The ECB has cut rates for eight meetings in a row (including today's cut), and Trump believes it is time for the Fed to follow suit. He has repeatedly called Powell “too slow,” but is that really the case?

The Fed is not known for cutting rates at the right time; on the contrary, it usually goes into panic-cutting mode during a recession. However, on this occasion, I believe Powell's approach is the right one.

The US has never taken such a clear stance on tariffs in recent history, and this could clearly have repercussions on inflation. For now, everything seems under control, but we need to wait a little longer to say with certainty that tariffs will not impact the inflation rate. Personally, I believe we will be able to draw conclusions at the end of the summer, so it is still too early. Consequently, I agree with the Fed's view and think it is better to wait a little longer before starting to cut rates.

Inflationary spirals do not occur overnight, or even from one month to the next; it takes time for them to spread throughout the economy. It starts with a few companies with inelastic demand raising their prices, then the phenomenon spreads on a large scale. Initially, the price increase is reflected only in products subject to tariffs, but then it extends to all others.

For a matter of credibility, the Fed cannot risk lowering rates now in the hope that tariffs will not generate inflation. Credibility is everything for a central bank, and the Fed cannot afford to send mixed signals about its monetary policy. Imagine a scenario in which inflation starts to rise in July/August and the Fed has already lowered rates: what does it do then? Raising rates again would be a clear signal that it has no idea what is going on. Better to make a few good moves than many confused ones.

The New York Fed recently released a business survey covering about 200 manufacturing and service companies in the New York-Northern New Jersey area. I find the results very interesting in understanding why the Fed is postponing rate cuts.

The New York Fed survey

The first important slide in this survey shows the number of companies that will pass on the increased costs due to tariffs to consumers.

As you can see from this image, approximately 38% of the companies surveyed passed on the entire cost of the tariffs to consumers; 24% absorbed the entire cost, accepting a reduction in profit margins. The extremes of this survey are significant: 62% of companies are either placing a heavy burden on consumers or on their profit margins. In my opinion, this result shows a clear disparity in how tariffs affect companies. Those with a strong competitive advantage have no problem passing them on to consumers; less solid businesses are becoming even weaker because they have to bear the full cost of the tariffs. In between, there is a well-distributed 38% that is trying to adopt a hybrid approach to overcome the current uncertainty.

In the coming months, I expect the distribution of data to be more skewed to the right. The 38% of companies that have chosen to increase prices by the amount of the tariffs are providing the initial trigger for inflation to rise. Companies that have not followed suit may do so as soon as they see their costs rise too much, triggering an inflationary spiral. Those who have not increased their prices may be forced to do so. Why should others raise their prices and I don't? That is the question they will ask themselves.

Clearly, the companies in this survey represent a small sample that is not representative of the entire US economy. The true value of tariff pass-through could be higher or lower than this. As far as I am concerned, I don't think it's too different, as I read every day about companies that have started to raise prices. Although we do not know the exact magnitude of the trend, we know that it is happening.

Let's now look at the second slide, which shows how quickly tariffs have led to price increases by companies.

58% increased prices within a month from the introduction of the tariffs; the remaining 42% took longer or are still considering a price increase. Based on the sample analyzed, it would appear that price increases due to tariffs are not yet widespread. Considering that in the 1-3 month sample there are companies that haven't raised prices yet (it hasn't been 3 months since Liberation Day), I'd say that about 50% of companies have already raised prices; the other half still haven't. Also, as we saw earlier, not all the companies that raised prices were able to pass on the tariffs entirely to consumers. Therefore, it is still too early to judge the effects of the tariffs. Companies are still thinking about the best strategy: once pre-tariff inventories have been exhausted, they will have to make a choice.

Finally, the last slide takes into account how companies' operations are changing due to the tariffs.

In this case, there are two interesting factors to note:

  • The first is that a quarter of companies are countering tariffs by increasing prices even for goods/services not related to them. This reflects a very common process during periods of inflation, as companies almost never limit themselves to increasing the price of individual products/services. The increase is widespread, as companies have to cope with higher costs due to factors such as wage and rent increases. In addition, about a third of companies are increasing inventory levels because they fear supply chain shocks or tariff increases.
  • The second factor that caught my attention is the deterioration of capital investment in the US by service companies; manufacturing companies are not faring much better. Trump sees tariffs as a way to get more companies to invest in the US, but for now, it's having the opposite effect. As for reducing imports, his plan is working, with almost a third cutting imports. But there's a problem. This has not been followed by an increase in imports from new non-US sources, so companies that import less have not found new trading partners to replace their previous ones. In other words, I expect those who have reduced their imports from China (the country most affected by tariffs) to have had difficulty finding another country to import from. The icing on the cake is that headcount is slightly down.

Companies seem to agree on one thing: tariffs are hurting the net income of many of them. This response was given by almost 50% of the companies surveyed and was the one with the clearest result. Of course, there are also companies that are not experiencing too many problems thanks to their competitive advantage (they can raise prices due to the inelastic demand for their products/services), but they can trigger an inflationary spiral and affect others.

Conclusion

Overall, the New York Fed survey highlights three interesting considerations:

  • Not all companies have started to raise prices.
  • Among the companies that are raising prices, there are also goods/services not directly related to tariffs.
  • Trump wanted tariffs to increase investment in the US, employment, and corporate profits; so far, this is not happening.

In light of all this, the Fed's decision not to cut rates yet seems quite reasonable, and we will probably only be able to judge the impact of tariffs on inflation in late summer; before then, it is too early to say. These concerns may prove unfounded, but the Fed cannot risk losing its credibility by acting a few months early. In a chaotic phase such as the current one, the market needs certainty, and a Fed that lowers and raises rates in a matter of months is far from encouraging; it is much better "to be late” by 1–2 months and doing the right choice.

A fiscal policy less inclined to increase the deficit would in itself be a way to reduce rates, as bond vigilantes would see US debt as safer and yields would fall. But it is much easier to blame the Fed.

data.title: Genesco's Athletic Shift Is Mostly Empty Calories
data.description:
Genesco Inc. (NYSE:GCO) reported Q1 '26 (calendar Q1 '25) earnings results. It was a very mixed quarter. While comps were good for Journeys, gross profit did not follow, as the footwear being sold is increasingly expensive to source and sell. Traffic is still declining, weighing on store productivity and inventory efficiency.

The short-term picture remains very difficult. Although the stock trades at a fraction of the earnings the company could deliver in the 2010s, it's expensive compared to the profits it can generate now, and I still don't see signs of structural improvement. I maintain my Hold rating.

Q1 '25 results

Journeys up on athletic: The best data point this quarter came from Journeys, with comps up 8%. This is a deceleration from the double-digit growth in Q3 '25 and the +14% in 4Q24, but still a very good figure. According to the call, Journeys is still tracking close to 8% comps in 2Q, and full-year guidance still calls for mid-single-digit comp growth.

Expensive growth: The problem is that this growth is expensive. Journeys' improvement came not from traffic, but from selling fewer units of more expensive athletic styles. ASPs were up 12% at Journeys, but unit sales were down, and traffic declined. The company is shifting its mix from canvas and vulcanized shoes toward athletic styles, which tend to carry a lower margin even if they are more expensive.

Gross margin at the overall company fell 90 bps, and although we don’t have segment-level gross margin disclosure, operating losses were close to $15.2 million in the quarter at Journeys, barely improving from $18.8 million a year ago. Usually, when a store presents 8% comps, its operating margins jump over the roof because of operating leverage. I commented on this problem in my Q4 '25 article. This suggests that the more expensive brands being brought into Journeys (including performance-running names like HOKA and Saucony now) are not particularly accretive to profits.

There is also a risk of accumulating inventory in these higher ASP categories if fashion trends shift. Journeys is not an athletic performance footwear retailer, it sells these pieces to kids and teenagers because that's what's fashionable right now.

Capital costs are also higher: Inventories were up 15% YoY, or nearly $60 million in absolute terms, driven mostly by the higher ASPs of the merchandise. This means capital costs for carrying inventory have gone up significantly.

Schuh is still challenged: Schuh continues to struggle. Comps were up 1%, the same as in Q4 '25, but margin trends worsened. Gross margin fell due to elevated promotions, and FX tailwinds (weaker USD) helped topline, masking weakness in underlying performance. The segment posted a wider operating loss.

J&M and GBG are in trouble too: J&M sales were down 5%, with wholesale driving the bulk of the decline. The segment had been the company’s strongest profit contributor in Q1 '25, but is now barely breakeven. Genesco Brands Group is also under pressure. While 1Q26 was better than expected due to shipment timing, liquidation, and license renewals, the business is guided to decline in HSD for the full year.

Capital allocation adds risk: Despite the deteriorating fundamentals, Genesco repurchased $13 million in stock during the quarter and indicated it may continue in Q2. At the same time, net debt increased by more than $60 million YoY. This is a risky capital allocation strategy. A footwear retailer with such limited profitability should not be adding debt to fund buybacks.

Valuation not attractive

The company reiterated EPS guidance of $1.30 to $1.70 for FY26. That translates into ~$13 to $18 million in net income, based on a diluted share count of ~10 to 10.5 million shares.

With a market cap of ~$240 million, this implies nearly 20x earnings at the midpoint — far too high for a business with three underperforming segments, and a flagship banner (Journeys) that is shifting its assortment in a major way without meaningful margin or traffic improvement.

Some bulls may argue that $15 million in earnings is a fraction of what Genesco generated in the 2010s when net income routinely reached $50–$60 million. And that’s true. But I don’t believe those figures are relevant anymore. Retail has changed. Fashion has changed. Genesco Inc.'s model does not generate the same margins it once did. This is not just cyclicality, but rather structural.

Until I see real transformation in the business model and clear signs of sustainable margin expansion, I prefer to value Genesco on what it can do today, not on what it did a decade ago. I maintain my Hold.

data.title: The 4-Fund Trump Portfolio: One Month Returns, A Sign Of More To Come
data.description:
Before I get into the nitty-gritty of this article, I want to reiterate that I am a long-term investor and have been advising investors on SA, for many years now, to build a well-diversified portfolio on the foundation of a high-quality, low-cost S&P500 ETF like (VOO). To be clear, by "foundation" I mean the single largest holding. I base that recommendation on research that shows that the vast majority of ordinary investors - and even the majority of professional money managers - do not even attain the long-term total returns of the S&P500. I have advised investors to over-weight the technology sector, have some strong dividend-paying and dividend growth stocks, and add a smattering of sector-specific ETFs to buoy the portfolio during times of market stress and volatility, to have a strong cash position, and to own some gold bullion as an insurance policy for financial system black-swan type events. Furthermore, I have also advised investors to build such a portfolio using a top-down allocation strategy based on his or hers own personal circumstances (i.e. age, working/retired, goals, risk tolerance, net-worth, etc.). That is, when it comes to portfolio construction, "one size does not fit all". In addition, I have advised investors to maintain this kind of well-diversified portfolio through the market's up-n-down cycles, and to add to it during down-cycles. That's because research also shows most ordinary investors, and professional money managers, are terrible at timing the market because you have to be right twice: when to get out, and when to get back in.

All that being said, one might ask why I am writing a Seeking Alpha article on the one-month returns of my suggested 4-Fund Trump Portfolio (A Seeking Alpha Editor's Pick). That's because the past month has seemed like a dog-year eternity to me, full of TACO Trump's tariff-induced drama and extreme market volatility. It ended up with a huge month-of-May rally that gained back all of April's losses - and then some. The total returns of the major market indexes, as represented by the (VOO), (DIA), and (QQQ), are all now in the green for the year (see chart below). That said, I'd point out that April's losses were arguably a result of the tariff policies Trump unveiled on "Liberation Day" (April 2) and that the end of April and month of May rally was arguably due to Trump simply pulling back, or at least delaying, some of those very same tariff policies.

So, given the yo-yo'ing of the markets (and of Trump's tariff policies ...) over the past month, and especially given the strong snap-back May rally, I was really curious to see how my 4-Fund Trump Portfolio held-up - especially given that my expectations were for a weaker market.

The 4-Fund Trump Portfolio

Here is the suggested 4-Fund Trump portfolio:

Fund Symbol Exp. Fee Yield
Vanguard Cash Reserves VMRXX 0.10% 4.25%
SPDR Select Utilities XLU 0.08% 2.86%
VanEck Gold Miners Fund GDX 0.51% 0.75%
Fidelity Bitcoin ETF FBTC 0.25% 0.00%

As explained in my first article, the 4-Fund Trump portfolio shown above was designed for investors to devote a percentage allocation to it from within the overall portfolio management strategy I outlined at the beginning of this article. That is, it was up to the individual investor to choose how much he or she wanted to allocate to the 4-Fund Trump Portfolio (if any at all), and how much to allocate to each of the four individual funds within the 4-fund portfolio. For the purposes of this article, I will assume an equal (25%) weight to each of the 4-Funds and that we started out with a $10,000 aggregate allocation to the 4-Fund Trump portfolio. So, let's see how we did after the mad-month of May.

1-Month Returns

As of the close on June 4th, the 1-month total returns of the four individual funds in the 4-Fund Trump portfolio are shown below:

The yield on the Vanguard Cash Reserves Fund (VMRXX) went from 4.25% one month ago to 4.24% (a drop of 1 basis point) while the US Dollar Index (DXY) declined 1.28% during the same period. The U.S. dollar index is now down 8.5% just since the beginning of 2025 (see chart below). So, relatively speaking and with a global perspective in mind, American investors still lost money on the VMRXX fund (i.e. their U.S. dollars continue to depreciate on a global basis). However, part of the reason for having a strong allocation of cash in the 4-Fund Trump portfolio was to be able to take advantage of market volatility and add to core positions on big sell-offs (like the two big dips in April). Hopefully, many of you took advantage of the big dip. However, my analysis today simply assumes the investor left the original $2,500 initial position in VMRXX alone.

Glancing at the chart above, investors should also consider the fact that American big-tech companies, which get a large percentage of revenue from overseas, are set to be beneficiaries of the big drop in the U.S. dollar because their foreign currencies will generate more U.S. dollars upon repatriation.

In addition, for those investors who think the U.S. dollar won't go much lower from here, consider the fact that the U.S. dollar index traded in a range of 75-80 for many years as a result of the financial crisis of 2008-1009 (the "Great Recession"):

In my opinion, if Trump's economic, foreign, and social policies are "successful", the U.S. could once again be mired in another such period, and potential much worse considering the days of near 0% interest rates are clearly over as the rest of the world is no longer keen on funding America's huge federal deficit spending.

The SPDR Select Utilities ETF (XLU) continues to benefit from what I consider to be a long-term secular bull-market in the electric power generation sector in the United States driven by AI data-centers, EVs, clean-tech, and the overall "electrification of everything" theme. Despite a -1.75% haircut on Wednesday due to rising long-bond rates, the XLU ETF was +2.86% over the past month. On a YTD basis, the XLU ETF has returned 7% - significantly more than any of the major market indexes. As I said in my original 4-Fund Trump Portfolio article:

... it is my opinion that even the Trump administration cannot stop the need for a significant amount of additional incremental electric power generation capacity in the U.S. in order to meet the demand from the hyperscalers' build-out of AI data centers.

In addition to previous earnings reports by hyperscalers like Microsoft, Google, and Amazon, which clearly indicated that huge investments into AI data-centers are continuing, we are likely to get more support for my thesis when Broadcom (AVGO) releases its earnings after the close on Thursday. I hope some of you had an opportunity to read my recent Seeking Alpha piece, The Long Wait For Broadcom's "AI Trifecta" Earnings Report is Almost Over.

Indeed, just this week, Seeking Alpha reported that META (META) announced a big deal to buy nuclear energy from Constellation Energy (CEG). CEG is the #3 holding in the XLU ETF with a 7.8% weight. The stock was +14% in pre-market trading on Tuesday after the announcement of the META deal. However, cooler heads prevailed after investors considered that the 20-year supply deal for 1.1 GW of power doesn't even start until June 2027. The stock sold-off 4.3% today (Wednesday) and is now lower for the week.

By the way, META is a Broadcom XPU customer and will likely be on the receiving end of Broadcom's leading-edge XPU designs (using TSMC's 2-nm process) in the 2H of this year. Regardless, the XLU utilities fund is the #2 performing sector ETF this year - and a close second to the Industrial's ETF (XLI). XLU currently yields 2.76% and its next quarterly dividend distribution is scheduled to be paid this month on 6/25/2025.

Not surprisingly, the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) was the star of the 4-Fund Trump portfolio over the past month (+12.8%). That is, unless you decided to buy my alternative gold play - GDX's higher risk/reward sister-fund: the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) - which was up a whopping 18% over the past month. Note that the price of gold itself, after hitting a high of $3500/oz on April 22nd, was up less than 2% over the past month as there were multiple "wobbles" (for lack of a better word) in the long-bond market due to the on-again, off-again tariffs. Meanwhile, the bond market was apparently not too impressed with the supposed "big beautiful bill" the Republicans are trying to push through Congress after the non-partisan Congressional Budget Office, or CBO, estimated the bill will increase the U.S. Federal debt by a whopping $3.8 trillion by 2034.

As is typically the case, BitCoin went on a wild-ride over the past month, but after all was said and done the Fidelity BitCoin ETF (FBTC) posted an excellent gain of 9.5%. I remain strongly bullish on BitCoin considering the following:

  • Trump signed an executive order to establish a strategic bitcoin reserve and a U.S. digital asset stockpile.
  • Trump Media (DJT) is raising $2.5 billion to fund a "Bitcoin Treasury".
  • MicroStrategy (MSTR) continues to make significant BitCoin purchases on a weekly basis.

MicroStrategy buys during the past month:

  1. Week ending May 11th: Bought 13,390 bitcoins, average price of $99,856.
  2. Week ending May 18th: Bought 7,390 bitcoins, average price of $103,498.
  3. Week ending May 25th: Bought 4,020 bitcoins, average price not reported.
  4. Week ending June 1st: Bought 705 bitcoins, average price of $106,524.

Since the company has a buy-n-hold strategy, in my opinion, these purchases effectively take these BitCoins off the market (i.e. they effectively reduce supply). At pixel-time, MSTR now owns a total of 580,955 bitcoins at an average price of $70,023. At the current price of $104,984/BTC, that equates to an unrealized BTC gain of $20.3 billion.

Now, just because MicroStrategy continues to buy BitCoin does not necessarily mean the price of BTC will keep appreciating. However, considering the company's arguably proven buy-n-hold strategy, and combined with the Trump administration's clearly pro-crypto stance and its intent to significantly lower crypto regulations, I think investing in Bitcoin is a much less risky proposition than many investors may believe it to be.

In addition, consider that since my first article on BitCoin was published on Seeking Alpha back in November 2020, the Grayscale Bitcoin Trust (GBTC) has outperformed the S&P500 by 367% (see: Bitcoin Penetrates Further Into The 'Mainstream' - It Should Penetrate Your Portfolio Too):

At the time that article was written, GBTC (a trust) was one of the few ways investors could easily and directly invest in BitCoin. Times have changed, and now there are a plethora of BitCoin ETFs and I strongly prefer the Fidelity FBTC ETF over GBTC - if for no other reason, because FBTC's expense fee of 0.25% is a whopping 125 basis points lower than GBTC's 1.50% charge.

So, now let's see where we stand with the 4-Fund Trump Portfolio after one month and knowing that we started out with $2,500.00 in each of the four funds:

Fund Symbol Yield

1-month

Total Return

Ending

Value
Vanguard Cash Reserves VMRXX 0.31% $2,507.75
SPDR Select Utilities XLU 3.32% $2,583.00
VanEck Gold Miners Fund GDX 12.77% $2,819.25
Fidelity Bitcoin ETF FBTC 9.52% $2,738.00
TOTAL VALUE $10,648.00

The month-end value of the portfolio now stands at $10,648 - a monthly total return of 6.48% (77.76% on an annual basis). However, let's not start popping champagne corks just yet ... one month does not make an investment strategy make and there is absolutely no telling what twists-n-turns await us, especially with respect to Trump's seemingly endless chaotic and erratic flip-flops on tariff policies and his inability to close tariff agreements with the major players: China, the EU, and India - for example. While Trump recently said that "it's hard to make a deal" with China's Xi, but so far, that has been the case with practically all the countries. In fact, the only concrete tariff agreement announced so far was with the United Kingdom, but there are still no details even on that "deal". Not to mention that the U.S. actually has a trade surplus with the UK, so in my opinion, that was a pretty much a non-event anyway. The reality is that foreign countries aren't as so readily bending to Trump's pressure as are the Republicans in Congress, and that appears to be a big surprise to Trump - but should not have been. Indeed, "Team Trump" recently said that they need to somehow make "90-deals in 90-days", which in my opinion is virtually impossible. The point here is that Trump's chaotic tariff agenda - and the associated extreme macroeconomic uncertainty it is causing - is very far from over.

Summary & Conclusion

In my opinion, there still exists massive macroeconomic uncertainty due to a significant amount of investment capital being put on-hold while CEOs wait for some kind of resolution and clarity about where Trump is leading the U.S. with his economic, foreign, and social policies (see The Trump Tariffs Are Paralyzing Business Investment). That being the case, it is my belief that Trump's policies will continue to erode American Exceptionalism rapidly (see American Exceptionalism: Gone In 90-Days). In fact, I would argue that since that article was published on Seeking Alpha, Trump has accelerated his war on American Exceptionalism as far as all the examples I cited in that article.

To preserve their wealth, I continue to recommend American investors - and even global investors for that matter - allocate a portion of their portfolios to directly benefit from a long-term secular decline in the value of the U.S. dollar. The 4-Fund Trump Portfolio was designed to do exactly that, and the portfolio's first one month's performance (+6.48%) was, in my opinion, outstanding and beat the pants off the returns of the broad major market averages. The first month's report card on the 4-Fund Trump Portfolio gets a grade of A+. I expect this portfolio will continue to significantly outperform the broad market averages throughout the rest of this year (and likely beyond) and continue to recommend investors BUY the VMRXX, XLU, GDX (or GDXJ), and FBTC ETFs.

data.title: 3 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Companies that regularly boost their dividends are typically seen as great investments in the long run, reflecting solid financial health and stability. Frequent increases in dividends imply that a company generates substantial cash flow, offering both income and total returns to investors.

I track dividend increases from strong companies and other relevant data points. I've gathered this information as part of my investment strategy and am excited to share my findings with my Seeking Alpha audience. This list is designed to help you make informed investment choices.

This week, I'll highlight more companies and present additional information about them, as well as the methodology behind the selection.

Here are some highlights about the group this week:

  • The average increase is 7.3%.
  • The average streak length is 11 years

My investment approach centers around buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that both pay increasing dividends and can also provide better-than-average returns (determined by beating a relevant benchmark).

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining two data sources: the “U.S. Dividend Champions” spreadsheet and upcoming dividend data from NASDAQ. This combination meshes data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company in this list has at least 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories, as I'll use them throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10–24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 0
Contender 2
Challenger 1

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Victory Capital Holdings, Inc. (VCTR) 6 3.13 10-Jun-25 4.26% Challenger
Hawthorn Bancshares, Inc. (HWBK) 14 2.75 13-Jun-25 5.26% Contender
Nasdaq, Inc. (NDAQ) 13 1.29 13-Jun-25 12.50% Contender

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date you need to own the stock.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
VCTR 0.47 0.49 4.26%
HWBK 0.19 0.2 5.26%
NDAQ 0.24 0.27 12.50%

Additional Metrics

Some different metrics related to these companies include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
VCTR 62.64 43.82 73.42 7.55 43% Off Low 15% Off High
HWBK 29.11 19.2 35.95 10.36 52% Off Low 19% Off High
NDAQ 83.86 58.28 84.15 23.56 44% Off Low 0% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the “Chowder Rule,” the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
VCTR 3.13 30.5 34.8 62.3 65.4
HWBK 2.75 12.9 11.4 13.3 18.5 16
NDAQ 1.29 9.1 10.1 8.9 17 10.2

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD boasts a strong performance record, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $68B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four main factors in the index that SCHD tracks.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

VCTR doesn't have a 10-year dividend growth rate, nor a 10-year public stock history, so I'm excluding them.

SCHD returned 176% over ten years, with dividends reinvested. Now, both companies that I'm comparing, HWBK and NDAQ, managed to outperform that benchmark.

I'm actually not surprised; they each have a 10-year dividend growth rate in the upper teens. That kind of compounding usually happens when earnings are growing strong. Stock prices also tend to follow high earnings growth.

NDAQ has been especially impressive with its total return. Both companies have had a history of outperforming SCHD to varying degrees.

Next Steps

I'm pretty picky when it comes to individual holdings in my portfolio. I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD, not just slightly beating it. Even from there, I want to see strong earnings and dividend growth over time. I also prefer to have an interest in the company, though not having a strong personal relationship isn't necessarily required (but it helps). These articles are my opportunity to review what has performed well and assess my current holdings as they provide their annual updates.

NDAQ has been a great performer, has been on my watch list for some time. That said, I hold 25 individual dividend growth stars, so the bar is set pretty high. At this point, it'll remain on my watch list, and I'll watch the story develop from the sidelines.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: SJB: Tactically Bullish As A Shady Credit Risk Environment Emerges
data.description:
Financial markets are in search of direction and the bond market is no different. Illustrating that claim is the fact that U.S. high-yield credit spreads have ranged between 259 and 461 basis points since the turn of the year. Moreover, real economic talking points surrounding both fiscal and monetary policy have seemingly struck fever pitch as many might argue that the "region of stability," has been breached.

Considering the current market climate, we think ProShares Short High Yield ETF (NYSEARCA:SJB) deserves coverage. As explained in the body of the analysis, we expect high-yield option-adjusted spreads to expand in late 2025 or early 2026, which, in turn places our outlook of SJB ETF in bullish territory.

Herewith is a deeper explanation of our outlook and why we think SJB ETF is well-aligned.

What Is SJB ETF?

The Fund's Optics

ProShares Short High Yield ETF seeks to -1x the daily return of the Markit iBoxx® $ Liquid High Yield Index, though longer-term returns may differ due to various technical reasons with transactional liquidity and basis risk being a few. Moreover, SJB ETF's returns are before-fee targeted, meaning investors will have to fork out a premium to gain exposure to the fund's managers.

The idea behind the concept is for investors to access a hedging tool when they think high-yield credit risk is unfavourable. This could be exceptionally valuable if you're unwilling to sell out of long-term positions. Furthermore, SJB ETF can be used as a short-selling mechanism for speculators or underwriters who'd like to offset their costs with fund-level distributions.

Strategically, SJB ETF uses derivatives to achieve its goals, whereby it engages in swap agreements. While not explicitly stated, we know these agreements as Total Return Swap Agreements, which is a lean way to engage in directional views, especially if you hold a collateral base of unencumbered securities. That said, unforeseen market events can lead to asymmetrical liquidity and higher collateral demands. Generally speaking, we're looking at a highly specialized strategy, substantiating SJB's gross expense ratio of 1%.

A view of SJB's total returns shows why it's generally suitable as a tactical ETF instead of a long-term position. Unlike its counterpart, HYG ETF (HYG), SJB has suffered negative returns since its inception, highlighting its used case interim exposure. Despite its negative long-run returns, SJB has shown sharp increases whenever credit spreads have dislocated (see succeeding chart).

Lastly, SJB has a distribution history. Although unsustainable, we think SJB's sparse distributions serve as a trading cost softener for retail investors.

The Index It's Shorting

SJB ETF shorts an index, which, according to S&P Global's (SPGI) material, is "designed to reflect the performance of USD denominated high yield corporate debt. The index rules aim to offer a broad coverage of the USD high yield liquid bond universe. The indices are an integral part of the global iBoxx index families, which provide the marketplace with accurate and objective indices by which to assess the performance of bond markets and investments. The index is market-value weighted with an issuer cap of 3%."

The fund's composition shows that it is sector diversified with a slight tilt towards the industrial and consumer services sectors. Furthermore, the index isolates the 1-to-10 year maturities, has a modified duration of 3.07, a weighted average coupon of 6.37%, and possesses broad issuer diversification, spanning around 1,240 issuers. In our view, we might see exposure adjustments throughout time, especially as economic cycle evolves. That being said, the index's weighting methodology means it will likely retain similar short-to-intermediate-term exposure.

Market Commentary

General Outlook For High-Yield Bonds

We are bearish credit risk, especially high-yield credit risk, for numerous reasons. Firstly, we deem the spread levels unattractive. High-yield spreads are below historical averages, which suggests docile excess return possibilities, likely introduced by investors chasing income for too long.

In essence, we deem the risk-return utility of high-yield bonds unappealing. In addition, we see spread widening occurring in due course, which we base on the following reasons.

  • Credit spreads can be mean-reverting – mean-reversion might be overdue.
  • The U.S.'s economic indicators are quite dicey and we expect these metrics to stay under pressure due to the U.S. arguably being in late-cycle, which has been perpetuated by an unstable fiscal policy environment. Moreover, geopolitical risks aren't abating, which adds pressure to global confidence levels.
Metric Current 1-Y Trailing
Quarterly U.S. GDP Growth -0.2% 1.6%
Leading Economic Index 99.4 101.7
Business Confidence 48.5 48.5
Consumer Confidence 52.5 69.1
Unemployment Rate 4.2% 3.9%
CapEx Index 27 20.1
10-Y CDS 48.54 circa 41.36 circa
PCE 2.1% 2.72%

Source: Trading Economics - Not All Metrics Are Measured In The Same Period, But All Are Y/Y Since Their Latest Recording.

  • Interest coverage ratios reached a bottom in early 2025, likely due to relief from interest rate cuts in late-2024. However, a loss in global economic confidence remains of concerns, especially for higher-risk borrowers. Moreover, we think tariffs will cut EBITDA margins of higher-risk companies that can't pass input cost rises to consumers. Collectively, we see fundamental headwinds emerging, especially in cyclical sectors.

A Model To Quantify Our Outlook

I built a model to quantify our views where I drew inspiration from Gilchrist and Zakrajšek, who, in 2012 delivered an option-adjusted spread pricing model, dictated by various economic activity factors and an anomalous factor. Our derivation didn't replicate the seminal model because Gilchrist and Zakrajšek performed numerous steps to prepare and fine tune the modelling process; instead, we merely drew inspiration from its pricing factors.

We used five variables, including GDP growth, inflation, fed funds rate, the yield curve's slope, and the VIX, to model high-yield OAS. Our model's output shows that it tracks quite closely to historical data with dislocations occurring from time-to-time. Our regression stopped on March 31st, pre-reciprocal tariff fiasco, communicating that option-adjusted spreads were likely underpriced at that stage (by about 100 basis points). Since then, we've arguably seen sustained economic risks, yet, the high-yield option-adjusted spread remains docile at around 319 basis points.

Side Note: The regressions smooths data to quarterly averages, meaning the OAS plotted below are interpolated and not point-in-time values.

The following charts supports the base regression by showing an adjusted R-squared of 68.3%, suggesting the model has strong explanatory abilities. Moreover, we used t-stats (explained here) to see which variables play a substantial role in pricing U.S. high-yield OAS. The VIX explains a lot but is difficult to predict. Furthermore, GDP growth, which is currently negative, explains a noticeable amount, and the yield curve's slope explains a statistically significant amount – remember, bullish yield steepening, usually suggests a recession has materialised, which is why it makes sense that the t-stat holds gravity in this instance.

The final piece of the model is the excess bond premium, which describes the portion of the OAS unexplained by default risk. The premium fluctuates quite substantially, yet negative values have been salient since the COVID-19 pandemic, suggesting default risk has dominated the spread in recent years, most likely due to fiscal dominance and unfamiliar monetary policies.

Kindly note the following limitations that pertain to the model:

  • Additional performance metrics need to be observed to paint a comprehensive picture of the model's accuracy.
  • The unexplained part of the model wasn't studied. The residual terms can form certain types of codependencies, biasing the explained part of the model's output.
  • Past relationships aren't necessarily perpetual. They might rhyme but not repeat.

How All This Plays Into SJB

You might've gathered by now that we expect option-adjusted spreads to move materially higher. In fact, we expect two things: 1) Spreads will move materially higher as we lead into the pronounced part of the credit cycle, and 2) occasional spikes due to the unstable political environment, led by narrative.

SJB is currently dislocated from HYG, where HYG ETF has drifted higher versus SJB since late 2023. Real economic growth has stayed solid during the earlier-stages of the dislocation period (and inflation cooled), likely justifying the move. However, we think it's time for convergence, where a change in real economic and market-based variables will lead the two ETF's closer.

Recent history shows three spikes in high-yield option-adjusted spreads during the past decade. If we reference B-rated OAS, a spike to around 650 basis points occurred in 2022 amid a stock bear market and bankin stability concerns; in 2020, we saw a spike above the 1100 basis point level when a structural shock occurred; and in 2015/2016, a level of circa 900 unfolded against the backdrop of global demand concerns. Correspondingly, I calculated that SJB's price rose by around 15.1%, 24.86%, and 10.33% during those periods (note: subject to rounding errors) – We believe we'll see a similar result materialise in late 2025 or early-2026.

Risk Factors (Very Important To Note)

Here are a few risk factors worth considering.

Firstly, SJB ETF can be exceptionally risky. Not just because of its negative return history but also because of its substantial max drawdown. Lower standard deviation is evident but standard deviation doesn't communicate the full impact of tail events such as stressed market circumstances.

Secondly, SJB faces a fundamental headwind: U.S.-based corporates are upping their reverse yankee issuances. In simple terms, U.S. companies have spotted issuance opportunities outside the U.S., particularly in Europe. As a consequence, we could see lower U.S. high-yield supply in the coming years, limiting the downside possibilities of high-yield bonds due to supply/demand imbalances.

Finally, coupons are quite high on the underlying index. The Markit iBoxx® $ Liquid High Yield Index has a weighted average coupon of above 6%, adding a cushion for those with long positions in total return swaps. To our understanding, short TRS' will require substantial price downside to combat the coupon effect, at least for now.

Conclusion

We think ProShares Short High Yield ETF provides a valuable tool for hedging a portfolio's short-term credit risk and/or shorting high-risk credit. Characteristically, SJB provides broad-based exposure and low modified duration, isolating the effect of systematic variables such as option-adjusted spreads and sector fundamentals.

We used a seminal research model and headline economic indicators to conclude that high-yield option-adjusted spreads were materially underpriced at the close of March. Since then, economic risk has arguably intensified, yet, spreads have yet to catch-up. Moreover, we believe interest coverage ratios will inevitably reflect the impact of tariffs and a potential loss of business confidence.

While factors such as an increase in reverse yankee issuances and bulky high-yield coupons can diminish SJB's interim outlook, we are net bullish, and have therefore decided to communicate our bullish outlook on SJB ETF with a horizon of 6-to-12 months.

data.title: The Negative Rates Of Change Are Mounting, Be Prepared
data.description:
When markets dislocate from fundamentals in a momentum-driven melt up, all news is good news, because pundits must come up with reasons each day why stock prices are rising. Yesterday, a dismal ADP employment report and ISM survey indicating the service sector is starting to contract was good news, because it may encourage the Fed to cut short-term rates. The day before a JOLTS report revealing more job openings than expected was good news because it suggests the labor market remains resilient. So, which is it? It doesn’t matter during short intervals like this. It works the same way in the opposite direction. When stocks were plunging day after day in March and early April, all news was bad news, even though it was not.

The difference that I see today is that in March the economic data was still improving, or at least not deteriorating, which gave me confidence we would see a rebound, whereas in recent weeks I have seen an increasing number of negative rates of change. That bore out in yesterday’s ADP payroll report, which showed the weakest number of additions in two years at 37,000. It is notable that the economy shed 3,000 manufacturing jobs. This sent bond yields plunging with the 10-year falling 11 basis points to 4.36%, helping support stock prices and lifting hopes that the Fed will lower short-term rates to stave off an economic downturn. The weak payroll report was followed by much softer service sector data.

The Institute for Supply Management’s (ISM) Services Index fell below the critical 50 level that serves as the line of demarcation between expansion and contraction. This was the first modest contraction at 49.9 since June 2024, but the sub-indexes were more concerning. The new orders index plunged from 52.3 in April to 46.4 in May, while backlog of orders fell from 48 to 43.4. At the same time, the prices paid index rose to 68.7, which is the highest level since November 2022, resulting in the largest two-month increase since March 2021 when the CPI was on route to 9%. Higher rates of inflation are coming.

S&P Global has a competing index, which came in stronger at 53.7 and still indicates modest expansion. That is encouraging, but input prices were at a two-year high, and price increases were passed on to clients to reflect the steepest increase in output charges since August 2022. Again, higher rates of inflation are coming. While domestic demand saw a lift, due to temporary tariff relief, foreign sales continued to decline. According to Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global, this report is consistent with GDP growth close to 1% in the second quarter.

Further supporting my expectations for stall-speed growth this year, the Fed’s latest survey of economic activity across its 12 districts, otherwise known as the Beige Book, indicates weakness was widespread in May. Nine of the 12 districts reported no growth or a contraction in activity, while labor markets were described as “flat,” and business contacts indicated the consumers were pulling back on spending. This report will have the Fed’s attention and would be a precursor to a rate cut if not for the inflation that is building in the pipeline and will start to show up this summer.

Inflation is a lagging indicator, and tariffs take time to work their way through the economy from producer to consumer. As they do, I think we will see negative rates of change continue to mount, a decelerating rate of economic growth, and gradual uptick in consumer price inflation. This will likely keep the Fed on hold this summer until officials have a better grasp of how tariffs and trade policy are impacting the economy. Perhaps the S&P 500 can continue to levitate in the face of this downturn, but it seems more likely that we will have a summer pullback, if not another correction, to account for the uncertainty during these oncoming headwinds.

Manager Commentary

“U.S. economic data started to weaken in February, and there is a lot of uncertainty. We are inclined to be buyers, as there are a lot more undervalued stocks than there were in the beginning of the year.”

Byron Place Capital Management

Market in Review

U.S. economic data started to soften in February, and small-cap stocks began to come under pressure in mid-February. The sell-off worsened as tariff fears became reality. Turnover in the exchange-traded fund (ETF) continued to increase, as we tried to reduce the exposure to some of our largest positions that have performed so well over the past six months but had become more stretched from a valuation perspective. Top 10 concentration in the ETF decreased from 45% at year-end to almost 40% at quarter-end. We actively repositioned the ETF, as evidenced by the last 12 months’ turnover rate moving up to 54.78%. In retrospect, we should have been even more aggressive selling, as stocks sold off indiscriminately in March. We could not find enough new names that met our strict underwriting criteria from a risk/reward standpoint. Going forward, we expect to be actively taking advantage of the elevated volatility in the stock market. While we can't predict the future, we wouldn't be surprised if the ETF took on more beta at some point, as value stocks look a lot cheaper, and we might be inclined to buy on fear-and-sell safety.

Portfolio Performance

During the first quarter of 2025, the Harbor Active Small Cap ETF (“ETF”) returned –5.43% (NAV), outperforming the Russell 2000® Index, which returned –9.48%.

Relative performance was very strong. We believe stock selection was the key, but factors that worked in our favor for the quarter were larger-size bias, higher return on equity, and non-earners underperforming. Value outperformed growth during the quarter, which could be looked at as headwind from a factor standpoint since we are currently a bit growthier than the index.

Shares are bought and sold at market price not net asset value (NAV). A fund’s NAV is the sum of all its assets less any liabilities, divided by the number of shares outstanding. Market price returns are based upon the closing composite market price and do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Contributors and Detractors

During the quarter, SS&C Technologies contributed to performance, as its share price was up 10.56%. The company reported higher-than-expected organic revenue growth in the fourth quarter of 2024. This is the type of small cap that may deliver relative outperformance in a downturn, given its defensive characteristics (larger size, stable cash-flow generation, and lower beta). There is no change in our thesis for buying and holding this company.

Kinsale Capital Group’s stock price was up 5.35% during the quarter, as results came in as expected. Similar to SS&C, Kinsale tends to deliver relative outperformance in a downturn, given its defensive characteristics (lower beta, larger size, insurance viewed as less economically sensitive). We are still fans of the stock but will watch its valuation and could foresee trimming at some point to buy more down-and-out names as the downturn potentially intensifies. This is a company that benefits from the flight to safety.

Beacon Roofing Supply’s stock price was up 21.78% during the quarter. The company agreed to be acquired by QXO at a premium. Subsequent to quarter-end, we exited the position. We are not in the arbitrage business and think there could be some risk to the deal falling through, if economic data continue to worsen and QXO gets cold feet.

Liberty Energy detracted from performance, as the stock was down 19.52% during the quarter. The company continues to execute and is the best operator in the fracking business, in our opinion. CEO Chris Wright leaving to become head of the Department of Energy was a slight negative, but we believe in the management team. We like the fact that the company is moving into the distributed power space, which is less cyclical. Oil prices have collapsed during the quarter, so it’s no surprise that this is the biggest detractor. This is a highly volatile, cyclical business and stock, and it is the only energy name we own in the ETF. We are inclined to buy more as we believe the stock is significantly undervalued; however, we will be careful as timing is everything. Cheap can get cheaper, and this stock could go down another 50% before it recovers and realizes its value. We did sell some of the stock in January at $21 when oil prices were higher.

RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) — another high-beta name — was down 21.12% during the quarter. Hotel REIT stocks tend to go down in an economic downturn. The company is a strong operator and well-balanced capital allocator. The supply picture in hotels is the best it has looked in a long time. The stock is dirt cheap based on asset value but can go cheaper in a recession. We are buyers but will be responsible in the timing of our purchases.

The share price of Shift4 Payments (FOUR) was down 21.42% during the quarter. The company, a software payments and processor, was one of the big winners in the ETF last year. The stock became expensive as it has had explosive growth and has aggressively expanded overseas. The stock is now coming back to earth, as its acquisitive nature and aggressive expansion overseas have investors a little spooked with a potential recession looming. In addition, founder Jared Isaacman may potentially be leaving to run NASA, and that has some investor disconcerted. We think Tyler Lauber and the rest of the management team are fantastic but acknowledge losing a force of nature like Isaacman would be a negative.

Buys and Sells

Northpointe Bancshares (NPB) is a well-run national mortgage bank with a solid 25-year history as a private company that launched an IPO in mid-February significantly below the anticipated price range. The company was looking to raise capital to go on the offensive in the mortgage banking market, given the retrenchment by so many competitors over the past few years. We are optimistic about the opportunity and the risk/reward potential in the stock, given its cheap valuation, but need to hold back from purchasing just now, given our limited history with the company.

United Bankshares (UBSI) is a well-run Mid-Atlantic and Southeast regional bank that we have owned off and on over the past 25 years. The pullback in the stock price provided a reasonable entry point to initiate a position. The price-earnings (P/E) ratio is currently 12x, with a price-to-book value of 1.5x, and a dividend yield of 4.7%.

We have owned Toro (TORO) in small-cap ETFs for many years. The company has executed very well and began to command a lofty multiple given its excellent results. We thought the stock had become overvalued and decided to sell. The stock price is now down close to 50% from early 2023 highs, and the P/E multiple has been cut in half. We view this as a reasonable entry point but understand there may be some cyclicality and that robust growth is unlikely.

During the quarter, we sold NNN REIT (NNN). It was a small position; there was a management change; the stock was a beneficiary of lower interest rates; and we believe there is more credit risk in its portfolio than the company believes. We prefer Phillips Edison REIT.

We sold part of our position in Warby Parker (WRBY) at $27. It was getting too expensive. With the share price now at $15, in retrospect, we should have sold it all. However, we are fine with the stock at these prices. If it goes down a lot more, we will be buyers again.

We trimmed Waystar (WAY) in the $40s, as it got too expensive. The company is executing well, and we would buy more at lower prices.

We trimmed Shift4 Payments at $115. With the price now in the $70s, we are close to buying more.

We trimmed Maplebear (CART) at a price over $50. With the price now in the high $30s, we will be buyers in the low $30s.

We trimmed Freshworks (FRSH) at close to $20 per share. With the price now in the $12s, that is in the ballpark of where we will buy more.

We have trimmed Doximity as it took off last year, and then trimmed again after it reported earnings earlier this year. The company is killing it, but it is a massive position and trades at over 40x earnings. We think it is fine here (in the low $50s) and will most likely stand pat with this position.

Outlook

U.S. economic data started to weaken in February, and there is a lot of uncertainty. We are inclined to be buyers, as there are a lot more undervalued stocks than there were in the beginning of the year. We are more likely to add beta and sell safety. Earnings estimates have come down and will most likely continue to come down. We are biding our time to get more aggressive.

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Stocks of small cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies. A non-diversified Fund may invest a greater percentage of its assets in securities of a single issuer, and/or invest in a relatively small number of issuers, and it is more susceptible to risks associated with a single economic, political or regulatory occurrence than a more diversified portfolio.

Benchmarks

The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the US equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® and includes approximately 2000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 2000® Index and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC is the Distributor of the Harbor ETFs.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Alpha is a measure of risk (beta) adjusted return.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

data.title: EPOL: Revisiting The Prospects Of Investing In Poland
data.description:
After five months of outsized positive returns, I thought it was time to revisit one of my more successful buy recommendations from this year. To be totally fair, I was right, and I was wrong at the same time. When I first wrote about iShares MSCI Poland ETF (NYSE: EPOL) on January 22, I noted that the World Bank had predicted that Poland would be one of Europe's fastest-growing economies in both 2025 and 2026. I also pointed out that the 31 stocks in the fund were trading almost at par with book value, with low earnings multiples when compared to their historical average. Since my article was published, the fund is up 31.2%. EPOL is actually up 45.1% year-to-date, making it the top-performing single-country ETF in 2025 and one of the top non-leveraged funds for any asset class.

So how was I wrong? In my conclusion, I compared EPOL to the iShares MSCI Israel ETF (EIS) which had gained 45% in 2024. I followed that reference with the statement that I did not see EPOL reaching that level of gains this year, but I thought “a return of 25 to 30%” seemed reasonable. I suppose I should not be too upset about my understatement of the potential for the fund.

With such significant gains in the books for this year already, it is time to check back to see what potential remains for 2025. Poland elected a new president, Karol Nawrocki, earlier this week, and his policies could change the landscape for some businesses in that country. Should investors cash out and reallocate into another opportunity, or are there still significant profits left to be collected in EPOL?

ETF Description

The iShares MSCI Poland ETF is the only available option for US investors seeking direct exposure to Polish equities. When doing my research for this analysis, I could not even find another ETF that had an exposure of more than 5% to Poland. The passively managed fund follows the MSCI Poland IMI 25/50 Index and currently has 33 total holdings. The portfolio is updated and rebalanced quarterly.

As mentioned in my first report, EPOL is heavily weighted in financials, but the allocation has decreased slightly. When I wrote in January, financial stocks made up 48.42% of the fund. As of June 3, that number is now 45.76%. The fund has also decreased its exposure to materials and communication companies by about 50 basis points. The largest increase in allocation belongs to energy stocks that now make up just over 10% of the fund’s portfolio. One thing that has not changed is the fund’s remarkably low exposure to the tech sector.

Below is a breakdown of the top 10 individual stock holdings from the fund website. Financial stocks compose three of the top four holdings and four of the top seven. Dino Polska SA (OTCPK:DNOPY), a Polish grocery chain that I profiled in May, is now the fund’s fifth-largest holding after being ranked sixth in January.

The valuations for the stocks in the fund have clearly increased faster than their profits this year. The published PE on the iShares website in January was 8.76. This week, that number stands at 13.19, an increase of just over 50%. The price-to-book ratio in January was 1.15. IShares states that it is now 1.42.

Multiples for international stocks should not be compared to their peers in the US because the growth and profit expectations for the average US equity are higher. Whether or not the current portfolio for the fund is overvalued by Polish standards depends on your perspective. Stocks on the Warsaw Stock Exchange have not been this expensive since 2021, but they are still trading below their 10-year average of 18.2.

While the share price appreciation for EPOL has been impressive this year, many people miss the fact that the dividend yield is also quite high, especially when compared to other broad index funds. In January, the fund had a yield of 6.03%. Today, that number stands at 4.21% because of the increase in the price of a share of the ETF. Dividends are paid twice a year, with the first payout of 2025 due later this month.

EPOL has an expense ratio of 0.60%. This is higher than the typical passively managed index fund, but reasonable considering the higher trading costs associated with trading foreign stocks. The other funds that Seeking Alpha lists as peers for EPOL all have management fees of somewhere between 0.5% and 0.62%.

The Impact of a New President

President-elect Nawrocki is scheduled to be sworn into office on August 6. Politically, his election is seen as a victory for the far-right. Nawrocki had never held any elected office previously, but he had the support of US President Donald Trump.

Most relevant for shareholders of EPOL are his expected economic plans for the country. Nawrocki’s conflicts with the EU over democratic standards and climate change may cause the country to lose access to EU funds which are currently supporting Poland’s economic growth. Disbursement of those funds, which totaled $156 billion, started in 2024. About $10 billion was approved at the end of last year to support green energy projects and boost transportation projects. A total of $68.5 billion has been earmarked for Poland so far, leaving the balance of almost $88 million still up in the air.

Other possible changes for Poland under its new president include a withdrawal from the European Green Deal, a reduction in taxes for Polish citizens, and an increase in defense spending. While tax cuts may free up more disposable income for Polish consumers, it could also constrain public spending on social programs and other public investments. The biggest takeaway from the research that I have done seems to point to a possible decline in confidence for foreign investors. As the country takes a step back from EU regulations, it risks losing the credibility that had been built in the last couple of years under the previous president.

Forecasts for the Remainder of 2025

In my opinion, two factors have supported the outsized rise in share price for EPOL in the first half of this year – merit and momentum. Merit would explain most of the rationale that I used in my January article. Momentum would explain the continued advancement over the last couple of months, as most investors saw the fund listed as a top performer on ETF screens. Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings support this idea as EPOL currently receives an “A+” momentum score.

However, I have noticed that some of my earlier merit arguments are starting to weaken. In addition to the valuation data that I mentioned earlier, there are predictions for Poland’s economy that are starting to move in the wrong direction. The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development, or EBRD, recently revised down its GDP growth projections for Poland for the second half of this year. The revision is small, moving from 3.4% growth down to 3.3%. Nonetheless, I keep thinking of a quote from American financial analysis Martin Zweig: "The trend is your friend, 'til it isn't".

The change in GDP projections from the EBRD was based on weaker demand for goods and services outside of the country, especially from neighboring Germany. The bank is one of Poland’s largest institutional investors, having allocated almost $1.5 billion into the country last year.

Much of the optimism for Poland at the start of the year involved the possibility of an expeditious end to the war in Ukraine. Polish companies stand to benefit greatly once reconstruction in Ukraine begins. The argument could be made that it was the start of the war in 2022 that caused Polish stocks to trade at such a significant discount.

However, just in the last month, tensions between Poland and Russia have escalated as Poland shut down the Russian consulate office in Krakow. The move was in response to alleged Russian involvement in a fire that was started in a Warsaw shopping mall last year. Russia responded by closing a similar Polish outpost in St. Petersburg. Unfortunately, there is no clear evidence to support that we are any closer to the end of the conflicts with Russia than we were at the start of the year.

Risk Assessment

When the war in Ukraine eventually ends, there could be some negative impacts for Poland in the short term. At the end of last year, about one million Ukrainian refugees were living in the country. Some analysts have estimated that these refugees may contribute as much as 2% to the growth in Poland’s GDP. How many of them will stay in Poland when the war ends is unknown, but there will likely be some migration back home. Labor force participation for Ukrainians in Poland is around 75%, about 15% higher than it is for Poles. A sudden tightening of the labor market could cause wage inflation to fill positions that have been recently vacated.

There are added risks associated with investing in any single-country ETF. Investors should be aware that EPOL has only gained 24% over the last 10 years, even though it has gained over 44% year-to-date. Seeking Alpha's Quant rating gives the fund a "D" rating which includes a "D+" for its heightened concentration in its top 10 holdings and a "D-" for its annualized volatility.

Part of the reason that EPOL has risen in value this year has been a decline in the value of the US dollar. The US Dollar has fallen over 9% in value in 2025 against the Polish złoty as shown in the graph below. Should this trend reverse course, the relative value of Polish equities would decrease.

Conclusion

Poland is a country that I visit often and has many cities that are near and dear to my heart. My own personal observations made during my trips over the last couple of years are what led me to begin researching this fund in the first place.

I still believe that the stocks included in EPOL represent a decent value compared to other international equities, and the high dividend yield that can be obtained with fund ownership should not be overlooked. Nonetheless, I am lowering my previous buy rating to a hold until I have a little more clarity about the possible impact of President-elect Nawrocki and the new polices that will soon be in place in the country. Regardless of its economic implications for Poland, I am also hopeful that a resolution to the Ukrainian-Russian war will come soon.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.description:
Introduction

Closed-end funds (CEFS) might not get the spotlight like mutual funds or ETFs, but they quietly hold $280 billion in assets, offering investors unique advantages. One such fund, Source Capital (NYSE: NYSE:SOR), has been in the game since 1968, giving us over 20 years of tangible performance data instead of theoretical projections. So, is SOR worth considering? Let's dive into the numbers.

The first CEF started in 1868, and currently, 449 CEFs are openly traded. Source Capital (NYSE: SOR) was incepted in 1968, so we have data for an investment that is longer than 20 years, and the data is not theoretical.

Portfolio Construction

FactSet classifies SOR as a large-cap CEF, but a closer look suggests it operates more like an asset allocation fund:

  • Focus on global large-cap equities
  • Invest in fixed income when no opportunities for equities exist
  • Investments are based on fundamentals with a focus on risk management.

These are the ten largest holdings in SOR’s portfolio and their allocation as of May 31, 2025. Unlike many funds, SOR avoids heavy concentration in single stocks, which helps mitigate volatility.

It does not do as well at controlling downside deviation (0.57 v. 0.65), but it does have a better risk-adjusted performance (10.05% v. 9.46%). Based on risk analysis versus SPY, this fund deserves a rating of Buy at best.

The simple allocation portfolios outperform SOR in almost every risk category. So, what is the problem if SOR has a better overall return? The problems stem from its rather high standard deviation (19.07%). Since this measure is used as a divisor for all of the risk measures, it lowers the results. At this point, it becomes a question of whether an investor is willing to stomach the sharper ups and downs this fund will have compared to its benchmarks.

My Take

Approaching SOR as a large-cap ETF like FactSet does will cause some confusion, which is why it is important to look at the underlying assets. Without doing so, one might be inclined to view SOR as a 3.5-Star fund. Its overperformance compared to the S&P 500 is appealing, even though the large deviation spread does cause concern.

So, how does one look at SOR? On average, it does minimize losses during down years (3.20% vs. -15.38%). That is an excellent result, but there is a rub. The worst 12-month period for SOR saw a loss of -55.13% (12 months ending 11/2008). That is a tremendous loss of capital for one to stomach for a moderately allocated portfolio, especially when the more traditional 70/30 or 60/40 portfolios limited their losses to half of that amount. On the same token, SOR saw a gain of 98.89% for the 12 months ending in March 2010. While one can say that was during a unique period in stock market history, these results are still part of SOR’s DNA.

SOR offers a compelling balance: strong returns, inflation resistance, and global diversification. However, its high standard deviation (19.07%) means investors must stomach sharper ups and downs compared to traditional allocation portfolios. If you prioritize growth and beating inflation, SOR is a solid pick. If minimizing volatility is your focus, a traditional 70/30 or 60/40 mix might be preferable.

Have fun and go make some money.
